planting.note: Plant fresh seeds indoors in warm, moist, fertile soil; water rose apple is frost-tender and suited only for zones 10–12 outdoors or container culture in zone 9 brought indoors before frost. Plant in fertile, well-drained to moist soil in full sun.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; water rose apple is a tropical Asian Syzygium closely related to the wax jambu (S. samarangense) and produces similar bell-shaped, hollow fruits that are crunchy, mildly sweet, and extremely refreshing with a high water content — as the name suggests. The fruits range from white to pale pink and are eaten fresh as a cooling fruit, particularly popular in Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines where they are sold by street vendors. The tree is medium-sized and can be kept more compact than many tropical fruit trees. It bears fruit prolifically in the tropics and is beginning to appear in specialty tropical fruit nurseries in Florida. In zone 9a, grow in a large container and move indoors in winter; the fruits are produced year-round in frost-free conditions.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; green sapote takes 5–10 years from seed but grafted trees fruit faster. More cold-tolerant than mamey sapote (to about 28°F) and can survive in the warmest microclimates of zone 9.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 9–11; green sapote is a Central American relative of the mamey sapote, producing large, round fruits with an olive-green skin and a rich, caramel-flavored orange flesh that is eaten fresh, in milkshakes, and in ice cream. It is less commonly cultivated than mamey sapote but produces equally delicious fruit and is somewhat more cold-hardy — able to tolerate brief dips to 28°F with some defoliation but surviving in protected locations in zone 9b. The fruits ripen in winter, providing fresh tropical fruit at a time when most trees are bare. In zone 9a, plant against a south-facing wall or in the warmest microclimate available and protect from frost when young. Grafted trees from named selections begin fruiting in 3–5 years.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; musk mallow grows as an annual in most of North America but perennializes in frost-free zones 10–12. In zone 9a it regrows from the base if frost kills the top in mild winters.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; musk mallow is a tropical Asian relative of okra and hibiscus grown primarily for the musky-aromatic seeds used in perfumery, liqueurs, and traditional medicine across South and Southeast Asia. The seeds contain ambrette seed oil, which has a rich, nutty-musky fragrance closely resembling animal musk — used as a natural, ethical alternative to animal musk in fine perfumery and as a fixative in high-end fragrance. The seeds are also used medicinally in Ayurveda and are added to the Egyptian spice blend 'beharat'. The large, showy, hibiscus-like flowers in yellow to red are highly ornamental. The entire plant — seeds, leaves, and roots — is edible, with the young leaves used as a cooked green. In zone 9, it behaves as a warm-season annual that may overwinter in protected spots.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; African yam bean is a long-day adapted tropical legume that fixes nitrogen and tolerates infertile, slightly acidic, sandy soils typical of West African savannas. Sow 2–3 inches deep.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun after danger of frost has passed; African yam bean is a twining annual legume from West Africa producing seeds with an extremely high protein content (23–29%) — one of the richest protein sources among African grain legumes. The seeds are a major staple and market commodity in Nigeria, Ghana, and surrounding countries, used in a variety of traditional dishes including moi-moi (steamed bean pudding), bean stew, and roasted bean snacks. A notable cooking challenge is the trypsin inhibitors in the raw beans, which require extended cooking or fermentation to neutralize — the seeds cook much more slowly than common beans. The vine is ornamental when trained on a fence or trellis. In zone 9a it grows as a warm-season annual from late spring through fall, producing a reasonable harvest in the long Texas growing season.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seeds after last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; drumstick sesbania grows naturally in wet ditches, pond margins, and seasonally flooded roadsides across the Gulf Coastal Plain. Scarify seed coat by soaking 24 hours in hot water before sowing.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun along pond edges, wet roadside ditches, and seasonally flooded areas; drumstick sesbania is a native Gulf Coast fast-growing shrubby annual to short-lived perennial — one of the most vigorous nitrogen-fixing native legumes of the wetland margins and wet roadsides of the Gulf South. The bright yellow flowers in summer are highly attractive to native bees, especially large bees and carpenter bees. The long, distinctive seed pods rattle when shaken — a characteristic of the genus that gives one of its common names. A native plant valued for quick erosion control and soil improvement on wet, disturbed sites. Note: seeds and foliage are toxic to livestock; do not plant in pasture settings. Native to the Gulf Coastal Plain from Florida to Texas.
